name: Drying and Preserving Food Part Three
recipeInstructions:
1. The moisture content of home-dried fruit should be about 20 percent. When the fruit is taken from the dehydrator, the remaining moisture may not be evenly distributed due to size or location in the dehydrator. Conditioning equalizes moisture distribution, reducing mold risk. To condition, pack cooled dried fruit loosely in jars and seal them for 7-10 days, shaking daily to separate pieces and check for condensation. If condensation forms, return fruits to the dehydrator for more drying. After conditioning, package and store the fruit.
2. Vegetables are preserved by drying, with drying time crucial for tenderness. Longer drying times result in less flavor and poorer quality. Drying vegetables until brittle (10% moisture) stops microbial growth. Prepare vegetables by washing in cool water, trimming, peeling, cutting, slicing, or shredding; remove fibrous portions, cores, decayed areas. Keep pieces uniform to ensure equal drying.
3. Blanching is necessary for some vegetables to stop enzyme action that causes color and flavor loss during drying and storage. It also sets color and shortens drying time by relaxing tissue walls for faster moisture escape/re-entry. Water blanching involves submerging vegetables in boiling water, while steam blanching heats them above boiling water with steam.
4. After blanching, briefly dip vegetables in cold water to stop cooking action but do not cool to room temperature. Drain vegetables onto a drying tray over the sink, wiping excess water from the underside of the tray, and arrange them in a single layer for drying in an oven or dehydrator. Vegetables should be brittle/crisp and contain about 10% moisture. They require no conditioning.
